name: Tobar Éinne (St. Enda's Well)
description: A holy well associated with St. Enda, founder of the great monastic settlement on Inishmore. Pilgrims still leave tokens at the surrounding stones.
folklore: Local tradition holds that water from the well, taken before sunrise, cures eye ailments.

name: Dún Aonghasa
description: A spectacular prehistoric stone fort perched on a 100m cliff above the Atlantic. Three massive concentric walls and a chevaux-de-frise of jagged stones guard the landward approach.
folklore: Said to have been built by the Fir Bolg king Aonghus, who chose the cliff edge so no enemy could ever come at his back.
nature: The clifftop hosts nesting fulmars and is rich in salt-tolerant maritime flora.

name: Kilronan (Cill Rónáin)
description: The main village and harbour of Inishmore. Ferries from Rossaveel and Doolin dock here; pubs, cafés and the heritage centre are within a few minutes' walk.

name: Joe Watty's Bar
description: A landmark pub above Kilronan, known for trad sessions, chowder and a sweeping view down to the pier.

name: The Worm Hole (Poll na bPéist)
description: A near-perfect rectangular pool cut into the limestone shelf below the cliffs — entirely natural, despite its quarried appearance. Reached by a rough walk across the karst.
folklore: Said to be the lair of a great sea-serpent (péist) that once terrorised fishermen.
nature: The surrounding limestone pavement is a textbook example of karst weathering, with grikes hosting ferns and gentians.

name: Dún Chonchúir
description: An oval stone fort crowning the highest point of Inishmaan, with commanding views across all three islands.

name: Cill Cheannannach
description: A ruined early-medieval church near the eastern shore, traditionally associated with St. Gregory.

name: O'Brien's Castle (Caisleán Uí Bhriain)
description: A 14th-century tower house built inside a much older ring fort, dominating the skyline of Inisheer.

name: An Trá Mhór
description: The wide, sheltered white-sand beach next to the Inisheer pier — safe for swimming on calm days.
nature: Shoreline dunes support marram grass and occasional sightings of grey seals offshore.

name: Plassey Wreck
description: The rusting hulk of the MV Plassey, driven ashore in a 1960 storm and famously featured in the opening credits of Father Ted.
folklore: All crew were saved by the islanders using a breeches buoy — a rescue still celebrated locally.
